meta.title: PMP Exam Prep — PMBOK Guide 8th Edition
meta.primarySource: PMBOK_8th_Edition_Breakdown.md (verified study guide) + PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. (ANSI/PMI 99-001-2025)
meta.note: Items tagged ecoExtended:true go beyond book-citable PMBOK 8 (agile/People/Business exam content). EVM formulas cite the PMI EVM convention.

formulas.name: Schedule Variance (SV)
formulas.interpretation: Positive = ahead of schedule; negative = behind; 0 = on schedule.

formulas.name: Cost Variance (CV)
formulas.interpretation: Positive = under budget; negative = over budget; 0 = on budget.

formulas.name: Schedule Performance Index (SPI)
formulas.interpretation: >1 ahead of schedule, <1 behind, =1 on schedule.

formulas.name: Cost Performance Index (CPI)
formulas.interpretation: >1 under budget, <1 over budget, =1 on budget.

formulas.name: EAC — atypical variance
formulas.interpretation: Use when current variance is a one-off; remaining work performed at the budgeted rate.
formulas.source: PMI EVM convention; Study Guide §3.3 'Schedule Forecasts'

formulas.name: EAC — typical/CPI
formulas.interpretation: Use when current cost performance (CPI) is expected to continue. Most common default.
formulas.source: PMI EVM convention; Study Guide §3.4 EVM

formulas.name: EAC — new estimate
formulas.interpretation: Use when original estimate is fundamentally flawed; re-estimate remaining work.

formulas.name: EAC — CPI × SPI
formulas.interpretation: Use when both cost and schedule performance affect remaining work.

formulas.name: Estimate to Complete (ETC)
formulas.interpretation: Expected cost to finish the remaining work.

formulas.name: Variance at Completion (VAC)
formulas.interpretation: Positive = expected under budget at finish; negative = expected overrun.

formulas.name: TCPI (to BAC)
formulas.interpretation: Cost efficiency needed on remaining work to hit the original budget. >1 = harder than planned.

formulas.name: TCPI (to EAC)
formulas.interpretation: Use once the budget (BAC) is no longer attainable and management approves a new EAC.

formulas.name: Three-Point (PERT / Beta)
formulas.interpretation: Weighted estimate; M (most likely) weighted 4×. Triangular variant = (O+M+P)÷3.

formulas.name: Expected Monetary Value (EMV)
formulas.interpretation: Used in decision-tree/quantitative risk analysis; threats negative, opportunities positive.

flashcards.back: A temporary initiative in a unique context undertaken to create value. (Changed from 'temporary endeavor' in prior editions.)
Application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to meet or EXCEED the intended value — not just meet constraints.
The excess of monetary and nonmonetary benefits over investment, gained from achieving project goals. Perceived differently by different stakeholders.
Output = a product/result/service generated by a process. Outcome = the long-term effect or change the output enables (benefits or disbenefits).
Program = related projects managed together for benefits not available individually. Portfolio = collection of programs/projects/operations managed to maximize strategic value.
EEFs = Enterprise Environmental Factors (conditions outside team control: regulations, culture, market). OPAs = Organizational Process Assets (plans, processes, templates, lessons learned).
1) Adopt a Holistic View, 2) Focus on Value, 3) Embed Quality, 4) Be an Accountable Leader, 5) Integrate Sustainability, 6) Build an Empowered Culture. (Consolidated from 12.)
Proactive (Holistic View + Embed Quality), Ownership (Accountable Leader + Empowered Culture), Value-Driven (Focus on Value + Integrate Sustainability).
Order of preference for handling negative impacts: Compensate → Restore → Minimize → Avoid. Addresses ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance).
Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing. Replace 'Process Groups.' Always present; overlap and iterate; NOT project phases.
Scope, time, and cost are determined early. Best when requirements are clear/stable, high investment, heavy regulation, cost of iteration > value.
Requirements are highly uncertain/volatile and likely to change. Timeboxed iterations (1–4 wks). Combines iterative + incremental.
Iterative = refine through repeated cycles (improve the same thing). Incremental = deliver in small usable segments (add pieces). Adaptive combines both.
Combines predictive + adaptive elements; useful under requirement uncertainty. Most real-world projects. Can vary by deliverable.
When budget and schedule are FIXED, scope becomes the variable. (Opposite of predictive, where scope is fixed and time/cost flex.)
Single (all at end), Multiple (components at different times), Periodic (fixed regular schedule), Continuous (ongoing incremental to production).
A review at the end of a phase: decide to continue, continue with modification, or end the project. Also called stage gate / gate review / decision point.
Structured = sponsor + PMO + governance board + PM (predictive). Self-governance = team autonomy with shared objectives + feedback (adaptive).
Leading = predictive, warn before tolerance breached (backlog size, engagement). Lagging = retrospective, measure past (SV, CV, deliverables done).
Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, Time-bound — used to set clear project goals.
Output of Initiate Project or Phase. Formally authorizes the project and grants the PM authority; links to business case & strategy. Adaptive charters are lighter.
Corrective action (realign to plan), Preventive action (avoid future variance), Defect repair (fix a nonconformity). Routed via change control / CCB (predictive) or backlog (adaptive).
Formally chartered group that reviews, approves, delays, or rejects change requests and records/communicates decisions. Predictive governance.
Project scope = the WORK performed to deliver. Product scope = the FEATURES/functions of the deliverable itself.
Approved Scope Statement + WBS + WBS Dictionary. Part of the Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) with schedule & cost baselines.
Work Breakdown Structure: hierarchical decomposition of total scope into work packages. Lowest level = work package (cost/effort/duration estimated).
NEW in 8th Ed. Hierarchical structure connecting scope to intended value; assigns each deliverable a value estimate; used to prioritize & estimate drag cost.
Dynamic, prioritized list of work items in adaptive environments — the adaptive equivalent of a WBS.
A checklist of all criteria a deliverable must meet to be considered ready for customer use. Common in adaptive.
Validate Scope = formal customer ACCEPTANCE of deliverables. Control Quality = checking CORRECTNESS against requirements. QC precedes validation.
Uncontrolled expansion of product/project scope without adjustments to time, cost, and resources.
Links each requirement to its origin and to the deliverables that satisfy it; ensures every requirement is addressed and tested.
Identifies the longest path through the network = shortest possible project duration. Activities on it have zero total float.
Precedence Diagramming Method: Finish-to-Start (FS, most common), Start-to-Start (SS), Finish-to-Finish (FF), Start-to-Finish (SF, rare).
Lead = overlap that lets a successor start early (acceleration). Lag = forced delay between activities.
Crashing = add resources to shorten duration (increases COST). Fast tracking = do sequential activities in parallel (increases RISK).
Leveling = adjust schedule for resource constraints; CAN change the critical path/end date. Smoothing = optimize within float; does NOT change end date.
Progressive elaboration of the schedule: plan near-term work in detail, future work at a higher level. Refine as info emerges.
Duration = calendar work periods to complete. Effort = labor units (e.g., person-hours) required. They differ when resources are added/shared.
Adaptive schedule tracking. Burndown = work remaining over time (trends to 0). Burnup = work completed climbing toward total scope.
CapEx = capital expenditures (invest in long-term assets). OpEx = operational expenditures (day-to-day running costs). Affects funding treatment.
Contingency = for KNOWN-unknowns (identified risks), in the cost baseline, controlled by PM. Management reserve = for UNKNOWN-unknowns, OUTSIDE baseline, released by senior mgmt.
Approved time-phased budget EXCLUDING management reserve. Part of the Performance Measurement Baseline. EVM compares against it.
Analogous (top-down, historical, fast/less accurate), Parametric (rate × quantity), Bottom-up (sum work packages, accurate/slow), Three-point/PERT.
Budget at Completion: the sum of all budgets established for the work to be performed = the total planned project budget.

flashcards.front: EV, PV, AC — the three EVM inputs
flashcards.back: PV = authorized budget for scheduled work. EV = budgeted value of work actually done. AC = actual cost of that work. All in same currency.
SV = EV − PV (schedule). CV = EV − AC (cost). Negative = behind/over. Memory: variance = EV minus the other letter.
SPI = EV ÷ PV. CPI = EV ÷ AC. Index >1 good, <1 bad. Memory: index = EV divided by the other letter.
1) AC+(BAC−EV) one-off variance; 2) BAC÷CPI typical CPI continues; 3) AC+new ETC re-estimate; 4) AC+[(BAC−EV)÷(CPI×SPI)] cost+schedule.
To-Complete Performance Index = remaining work ÷ remaining funds. To BAC: (BAC−EV)÷(BAC−AC). To EAC: (BAC−EV)÷(EAC−AC). >1 = must improve.
VAC = BAC − EAC (forecast over/under at finish). ETC = EAC − AC (cost still to spend).
tE = (O + 4M + P) ÷ 6 (Beta). Triangular = (O+M+P)÷3. Beta std dev = (P−O)÷6.
Expected Monetary Value = Probability × Impact. Sum across branches in decision-tree analysis; threats are negative, opportunities positive.
Any individual/group/org that may affect, be affected by, or PERCEIVE itself affected by a decision, activity, or outcome. Can number in the millions.
Research consistently shows active sponsor engagement is one of the strongest predictors of project success.
Stakeholder classification tool. High power+high interest = Manage Closely; high power+low interest = Keep Satisfied; low+high = Keep Informed; low+low = Monitor.
flashcards.source: Power/Interest Grid per PMI standard (tool named in Study Guide §3.5; quadrant labels not in the guide — verify)

flashcards.back: Classifies stakeholders by Power, Urgency, and Legitimacy. Those with all three are 'definitive' stakeholders demanding priority.
flashcards.source: Salience Model attributes per PMI standard (tool named in Study Guide §3.5; attribute detail not in the guide — verify)

flashcards.back: Maps each stakeholder's CURRENT vs. DESIRED engagement (Unaware, Resistant, Neutral, Supportive, Leading). C = current, D = desired.
flashcards.source: Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix levels per PMI standard (matrix named in Study Guide §3.5; level labels not in the guide — verify)

flashcards.back: Output of Identify Stakeholders. Lists stakeholders with their interests, influence, interdependencies, and potential impact.
n(n−1)÷2 where n = number of people. Adding people raises channels nonlinearly. (Classic PMP calc.)
Centralized = top-down PM direction (predictive). Distributed = self-organizing teams, decentralized decisions (adaptive/agile).
Responsibility assignment: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (one owner), Consulted (two-way input), Informed (one-way updates).
Output of Plan Resource Management. Documents team behavioral norms, working agreements, ground rules, and values — created BY the team.
Resource manager has management authority over resources (matrix/functional). PM may not directly control resource selection — must negotiate.
Guide, develop, and manage the team for high performance: feedback, coaching, conflict resolution, escalation. Tools: EI, colocation, ground rules.
Risk = an uncertain (potential) event with positive or negative effect. Issue = a condition that has ALREADY occurred and needs action now.
Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept, Escalate. (Escalate when outside the team's authority/scope.)
Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept, Escalate. (Exploit = make sure it happens; Enhance = increase probability/impact.)
Appetite = degree of uncertainty an org will accept for a reward (qualitative). Threshold = the measurable variation around an objective that reflects appetite.
Known-known = facts (scope). Known-unknown = classic risks (contingency reserve). Unknown-known = hidden facts. Unknown-unknown = emergent/black swan (mgmt reserve).
Qualitative = probability × impact matrix, prioritize (always done). Quantitative = Monte Carlo, decision trees, sensitivity/tornado, EMV (only when warranted).
Secondary risk = a NEW risk created by implementing a response. Residual risk = the leftover risk REMAINING after a response.
Deliberate adaptation of approach, governance, and processes to suit the environment and work. Expected, not optional. Too few = ineffective; too many = wasteful.
1) Select initial development approach (suitability filter), 2) Tailor for the organization, 3) Tailor for the project, 4) Implement ongoing improvement.
Fixed-Price: seller bears cost risk. Cost-Reimbursable: buyer bears cost risk. T&M: hybrid, for staff aug / uncertain scope.
RFP (proposal/solution), RFQ (quote/price), RFI (information), IFB (sealed bid). Used to solicit sellers.
flashcards.source: Study Guide Appendix X4; Glossary 'bid documents'

flashcards.back: Governance decision to insource vs. outsource. Considers cost, specialized skills, risk profile, schedule, and culture.
Supportive (templates/consulting, low control), Controlling (compliance/governance, moderate), Directive (manages projects directly, high control).
Automation (AI executes tasks), Assistance (AI supports human decisions), Augmentation (AI enhances human capability). PM stays responsible for AI output.
Evaluate the full cost of doing business across Profit, People, and Planet. Underpins sustainability value.
Minimum Viable Product: the fewest features/requirements that still deliver value, defining the scope of the first release.
Adaptive metric: the amount of work (e.g., story points) a team completes per iteration; used for forecasting, not for comparing teams.
Flow-based method using an information radiator (board) and Work-In-Progress limits to expose bottlenecks and optimize flow.
Leader serves the team: remove impediments, facilitate, coach, shield from distractions. Core to agile/People-domain leadership.
Team development stages: Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, Adjourning. Helps the PM adapt leadership style by stage.
Collaborate/Problem-Solve = win-win, generally best. Others: Compromise, Smooth/Accommodate, Force/Direct, Withdraw/Avoid (generally worst).
Shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. Enables an empowered culture (Principle 6).
Self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, relationship management. A key Lead-the-Team tool.
Governance process: leverage existing knowledge + create new; contributes to organizational learning. Outputs include lessons learned register.
Explicit = codifiable (words, numbers, pictures). Tacit = personal, hard to articulate (beliefs, experience, insight). Manage Project Knowledge handles both.
Verify deliverables accepted, archive knowledge, release resources, document lessons learned, transition to operations. Adaptive: retrospectives + knowledge transfer.
Integrated scope + schedule + cost baselines used for EVM. Changes only via formal change control.
Oversight & coordination, Solicit/manage feedback, Facilitate & support, Perform work, Apply expertise, Provide direction & insight, Provide resources. (Functions, not roles.)
Authority rises from Functional (weak/none) → Matrix (weak/balanced/strong) → Project-Oriented (high). Matrix shares authority with functional managers.
Person responsible for maximizing product value; accountable for the end product; orders/prioritizes the backlog. Adaptive role.
Advisory body of senior stakeholders giving direction/support and making decisions outside the team's authority.
Performance, Conformity, Reliability, Resilience, Satisfaction, Uniformity, Efficiency, Sustainability, Compliance. Quality is part of the Scope domain in 8th Ed.
Conformance (prevention + appraisal) vs. Non-conformance (internal + external failure). Investing in prevention is cheaper than failure.
Validation = does it meet the customer's NEEDS (right product)? Verification = does it comply with the SPEC/requirement (built right)?
A highly visible display (e.g., Kanban board, burndown) that broadcasts project status to the team and stakeholders — promotes transparency.

questions.stem: In the PMBOK Guide 8th Edition, project management is defined as the application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to project activities in order to do what?
options: Meet the project's scope, schedule, and cost constraints
Meet or exceed the intended value
Deliver outputs within the agreed baselines
Complete the work authorized in the charter
questions.explanation: The 8th Edition redefines PM around VALUE — 'meet or exceed the intended value' — rather than simply satisfying constraints.

questions.stem: How many principles are in the PMBOK Guide 8th Edition, and what happened relative to the 7th Edition?
options: 8 principles, aligned to the performance domains
10 principles, newly created for the 8th Edition
questions.explanation: The 7th Edition's 12 principles were consolidated into 6 more actionable, less overlapping principles.

questions.stem: The Sustainability Pyramid lists the preferred order for handling negative project impacts. What is that order?
questions.explanation: The pyramid escalates from least to most preferable: Compensate → Restore → Minimize → Avoid negative impacts.

questions.stem: Which statement about the Five Focus Areas is correct?
options: They are sequential project phases that occur once each
They replaced Process Groups and can overlap, iterate, and run simultaneously
They are the same as the seven performance domains
Monitoring & Controlling occurs only after Executing ends
questions.explanation: Focus Areas replace Process Groups; they always exist, overlap, and iterate. They are NOT phases, and M&C runs in parallel with the others.

questions.stem: A project has clear, stable requirements, heavy regulatory oversight, and a very high cost of rework. Which development approach is the best initial fit?
options: Predictive, because requirements are stable and iteration is costly
questions.explanation: Predictive fits when requirements are clear/stable, regulation is heavy, and the cost of iteration exceeds its value.

questions.stem: In adaptive projects following the 'inverted triangle,' when budget and schedule are fixed, which constraint becomes variable?
questions.explanation: With time and cost fixed (timeboxed), scope flexes — the team delivers the highest-value scope that fits.

questions.stem: Which pair correctly distinguishes leading from lagging indicators?
options: Leading = schedule variance; Lagging = backlog size
Leading = backlog size and engagement levels; Lagging = cost/schedule variance
questions.explanation: Leading indicators are predictive (backlog size, engagement); lagging indicators are retrospective (SV, CV, deliverables completed).

questions.stem: Which document formally authorizes the project and grants the project manager authority?
questions.explanation: The charter (output of Initiate Project or Phase) formally authorizes the project and grants the PM authority.

questions.stem: Midway through a predictive project, a stakeholder requests a significant scope addition. What should the PM do FIRST?
options: Implement the change to keep the stakeholder satisfied
Reject the change because the baseline is approved
Evaluate the change's impact and route it through the change control process
Add it to the next iteration's backlog
questions.explanation: PMP mindset: assess impact, then submit a change request through integrated change control (CCB). Never implement or refuse unilaterally.

questions.stem: Which change type is intended to PREVENT a future deviation rather than fix a current one?
questions.explanation: Preventive action addresses a possible future deviation; corrective action realigns current performance; defect repair fixes a nonconformity.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes project scope from product scope?
options: Project scope = features of the deliverable; product scope = the work to build it
Project scope = the work performed to deliver; product scope = the features/functions of the deliverable
They are synonyms in the 8th Edition
Product scope applies only to adaptive projects
questions.explanation: Project scope is the WORK; product scope is the FEATURES/functions of the deliverable itself.

questions.stem: Which artifact is NEW in the 8th Edition and connects project scope to the intended value of each deliverable?
questions.explanation: The VBS is new in the 8th Edition; it links scope to value estimates and helps prioritize and assess drag cost.

questions.stem: How does Validate Scope differ from Control Quality?
options: Validate Scope checks correctness; Control Quality gets customer acceptance
Validate Scope gets formal customer acceptance; Control Quality checks correctness against requirements
They are performed by the same person at the same time
Control Quality occurs only in adaptive projects
questions.explanation: Control Quality verifies correctness (internal); Validate Scope obtains the customer's formal acceptance. QC normally precedes validation.

questions.stem: Uncontrolled expansion of project or product scope without adjusting time, cost, and resources is called:
questions.explanation: Scope creep = uncontrolled expansion without adjusting time/cost/resources. (Gold plating is adding extras the customer didn't request.)

questions.stem: The scope baseline consists of which three components?
options: Charter, scope statement, and WBS dictionary
Scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary
WBS, schedule baseline, and cost baseline
Requirements documentation, WBS, and product backlog
questions.explanation: Scope baseline = approved scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary. With schedule and cost baselines it forms the PMB.

questions.stem: The critical path of a project is best described as:
options: The path with the most activities
The longest path through the network, determining the shortest possible duration
The path with the most expensive activities
Any path with positive total float
questions.explanation: The critical path is the longest path through the schedule network and sets the minimum (shortest possible) project duration; its activities have zero float.

questions.stem: A predictive project is behind schedule. The sponsor will fund extra resources but cannot accept added risk. Which compression technique fits?
options: Fast tracking, performing activities in parallel
Crashing, adding resources to shorten duration
questions.explanation: Crashing adds resources to shorten duration (raises cost). Fast tracking parallelizes work but adds RISK, which the sponsor rejected.

questions.stem: Which resource technique can change the critical path or the project end date?
questions.explanation: Resource leveling adjusts for resource constraints and CAN change the critical path/end date. Smoothing works within float and does not.

questions.stem: Which precedence relationship is the most commonly used in the Precedence Diagramming Method?
questions.explanation: Finish-to-Start (FS) is by far the most common; Start-to-Finish (SF) is the rarest.

questions.stem: An activity needs 80 labor-hours of work but two people can split it over one week. Which statement is correct?
options: Duration and effort are both 80 hours
Effort is 80 hours; duration is the calendar time (about one week)
Effort is one week; duration is 80 hours
Duration always equals effort regardless of resources
questions.explanation: Effort = labor units (80 hrs). Duration = calendar work periods (~1 week here). Adding resources can shorten duration without changing effort.

questions.stem: An adaptive team tracks progress with a chart showing work remaining trending toward zero across the iteration. This is a:
questions.explanation: A burndown chart shows remaining work declining toward zero; a burnup shows completed work climbing toward total scope.

questions.stem: In an adaptive environment, what serves as the equivalent of a WBS for organizing work?
questions.explanation: The dynamic, prioritized product backlog is the adaptive equivalent of the WBS.

questions.stem: A checklist of all criteria a deliverable must meet before it is considered ready for customer use is the:
options: Acceptance criteria for a single story
questions.explanation: The Definition of Done is the shared checklist that applies before a deliverable can be considered complete/usable.

questions.stem: During a sprint, a developer is repeatedly blocked waiting on an external team. What should the servant-leader project lead do FIRST?
options: Reassign the developer to other work and ignore the blocker
Work to remove the impediment by engaging the external team
Reduce the sprint scope without discussion
questions.explanation: Servant leadership prioritizes removing impediments for the team. The PM facilitates resolution before escalating. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: Which best describes a Minimum Viable Product (MVP)?
options: A fully featured release ready for general availability
The fewest features that still deliver value, defining the first release scope
A prototype that is thrown away after feedback
questions.explanation: An MVP identifies the fewest features/requirements that deliver value, scoping the first release for early feedback.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes contingency reserve from management reserve?
options: Contingency is for unknown-unknowns; management reserve is for known risks
Contingency is for known risks and is in the cost baseline; management reserve is for unknown-unknowns and sits outside the baseline
Both are controlled by the project manager
Management reserve is part of the cost baseline
questions.explanation: Contingency = known-unknowns, inside the cost baseline, PM-controlled. Management reserve = unknown-unknowns, outside the baseline, released by senior management.

questions.stem: The cost baseline is best described as the approved time-phased budget that:
options: Equals the sum of work package estimates only
questions.explanation: The cost baseline includes contingency reserve but EXCLUDES management reserve. BAC = cost baseline; the total budget = cost baseline + management reserve.

questions.stem: A project has EV = 400 and AC = 500. What is the cost variance (CV), and what does it indicate?
questions.explanation: CV = EV − AC = 400 − 500 = −100. Negative CV means the project is over budget (cost, not schedule).

questions.stem: A project has EV = 600 and PV = 750. What is the SPI, and what does it mean?
questions.explanation: SPI = EV ÷ PV = 600 ÷ 750 = 0.8. Below 1 means behind schedule.

questions.stem: BAC = 100,000 and the current CPI = 0.8 is expected to continue. What is the EAC?
questions.explanation: When the current CPI is expected to continue: EAC = BAC ÷ CPI = 100,000 ÷ 0.8 = 125,000.

questions.stem: Which EAC formula should you use when the current cost variance is considered atypical (a one-time event)?
questions.explanation: For a one-off variance, remaining work is assumed at the budgeted rate: EAC = AC + (BAC − EV).

questions.stem: BAC = 100,000 and EAC = 125,000. What is the VAC and what does it signal?
options: VAC = −25,000, expected over budget at completion
questions.explanation: VAC = BAC − EAC = 100,000 − 125,000 = −25,000. Negative = a projected overrun at completion.

questions.stem: BAC = 100,000, EV = 40,000, AC = 50,000. What TCPI is required to still complete within the original budget?
questions.explanation: TCPI to BAC = (BAC − EV) ÷ (BAC − AC) = (100,000 − 40,000) ÷ (100,000 − 50,000) = 60,000 ÷ 50,000 = 1.2. Above 1 means remaining work must be more efficient than planned.

questions.stem: If EAC = 125,000 and AC = 50,000, what is the ETC?
questions.explanation: ETC = EAC − AC = 125,000 − 50,000 = 75,000 — the cost expected to finish the remaining work.

questions.stem: Using PERT/Beta with Optimistic = 6, Most Likely = 12, Pessimistic = 30 days, what is the expected duration?
A risk has a 30% probability and a −$50,000 impact. What is its Expected Monetary Value (EMV)?
questions.explanation: EMV = Probability × Impact = 0.30 × (−50,000) = −$15,000. Threats carry negative EMV.

questions.stem: Which best matches the 8th Edition definition of a stakeholder?
options: Only those who fund the project
Anyone who may affect, be affected by, or perceive themselves affected by the project
Only the project team and sponsor
Only external customers and end users
questions.explanation: Stakeholders include anyone who may affect, be affected by, or PERCEIVE themselves affected — internal or external.

questions.stem: Research cited in the 8th Edition identifies which factor as one of the strongest predictors of project success?
questions.explanation: An active sponsor is repeatedly shown to be a critical success factor.

questions.stem: Which tool maps each stakeholder's current versus desired engagement level (Unaware, Resistant, Neutral, Supportive, Leading)?
questions.explanation: The Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix records current (C) vs. desired (D) engagement to reveal gaps to close.

questions.source: Salience Model attributes per PMI standard (named as a tool in Study Guide §3.5; attribute detail not in the guide — verify)
questions.stem: The Salience Model classifies stakeholders using which three attributes?
questions.explanation: Salience uses Power, Urgency, and Legitimacy; stakeholders with all three are 'definitive.'

questions.stem: A project team grows from 5 to 7 members. By how many do the communication channels increase?
questions.explanation: Channels = n(n−1)/2. For 5: 10; for 7: 21. Increase = 11. (ECO-extended calculation.)

questions.stem: A key stakeholder is consistently resistant and undermining the project in meetings. What should the PM do FIRST?
options: Escalate to the sponsor to have them removed
Ignore them and focus on supportive stakeholders
Meet with them to understand concerns and address the root cause
questions.explanation: Engage directly to understand and address the underlying concern. PMP mindset favors proactive engagement over avoidance or escalation as a first step.

questions.source: Power/Interest Grid per PMI standard (tool named in Study Guide §3.5; quadrant labels not in the guide — verify)
questions.stem: On a Power/Interest grid, how should a stakeholder with HIGH power and LOW interest be handled?
questions.explanation: High power / low interest = Keep Satisfied. High power / high interest = Manage Closely.

questions.stem: In a RACI matrix, how many parties should be 'Accountable' for a given activity?
questions.explanation: There should be exactly ONE Accountable party per activity, though there can be multiple Responsible, Consulted, and Informed.

questions.stem: Which artifact captures the team's behavioral norms, ground rules, and working agreements?
questions.explanation: The team charter documents norms, ground rules, and working agreements and is best created by the team itself.

questions.stem: Two senior engineers disagree sharply on a technical design. As the PM, which conflict-resolution approach is generally MOST effective?
options: Forcing — impose your decision to save time
Withdrawing — let them cool off and revisit later
Collaborating/Problem-solving — work toward a win-win using both viewpoints
Smoothing — emphasize areas of agreement and minimize the difference
questions.explanation: Collaborate/Problem-Solve seeks a win-win and durable resolution; it is generally the best mode. Withdraw/Avoid is generally the worst. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: A newly formed team is experiencing conflict over roles and approach. Which Tuckman stage are they in?
questions.explanation: Storming is marked by conflict over roles/approach. The PM coaches the team through it toward Norming. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: In a matrix organization, the PM needs a specialist who reports to a functional manager. What is the PM's BEST first action?
options: Direct the specialist to join the project
Negotiate with the functional manager for the resource
questions.explanation: In a matrix, the PM does not own the resource and must negotiate with the resource/functional manager before escalating.

questions.stem: Which leadership style aligns with self-organizing adaptive teams?
questions.explanation: Adaptive teams use distributed leadership with decentralized decisions; predictive tends toward centralized direction.

questions.stem: What is the key difference between a risk and an issue?
options: A risk has already occurred; an issue is potential
A risk is potential/uncertain; an issue has already occurred
They are the same in the 8th Edition
A risk is always negative; an issue is always positive
questions.explanation: A risk is an uncertain (potential) future event; an issue is a condition that has already occurred and needs action now.

questions.stem: Which set lists valid response strategies for THREATS (negative risks)?
questions.explanation: Threat strategies: Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept, Escalate. Exploit/Share/Enhance are for opportunities.

questions.stem: A project faces a low-probability but financially catastrophic risk the team cannot reduce. Purchasing insurance is an example of which strategy?
questions.explanation: Insurance shifts the financial impact to a third party — that is Transfer.

questions.stem: A identified risk falls entirely outside the project's scope and the team's authority. The BEST response strategy is to:
options: Mitigate it with the team's resources
Escalate it to the appropriate party in the organization
Ignore it as out of scope
questions.explanation: When a risk is outside the team's authority/scope, Escalate it to those who can own it — for both threats and opportunities.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly relates risk appetite and risk threshold?
options: Appetite is the measurable limit; threshold is the general willingness
Appetite is the general willingness to accept uncertainty; threshold is the measurable variation reflecting that appetite
questions.explanation: Appetite = qualitative willingness to accept uncertainty for reward; threshold = the measurable variation around an objective that reflects appetite.

questions.stem: Which technique belongs to QUANTITATIVE risk analysis rather than qualitative?
questions.explanation: Monte Carlo simulation is quantitative. Probability/impact matrix, categorization, and urgency are qualitative techniques.

questions.stem: Implementing a risk response introduces a brand-new risk that did not previously exist. This is called a:
questions.explanation: A secondary risk arises directly from implementing a response. A residual risk is what REMAINS after the response. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: In the known/unknown classification, which type is typically addressed with management reserve?
questions.explanation: Unknown-unknowns (emergent/black-swan) are covered by management reserve; known-unknowns use contingency reserve.

questions.stem: Which statement about tailoring in the 8th Edition is correct?
options: Tailoring is optional and discouraged for certified PMs
Tailoring is the deliberate adaptation of approach, governance, and processes — and is expected
Tailoring means always removing processes to save time
Tailoring applies only to adaptive projects
questions.explanation: Tailoring is the deliberate adaptation of approach/governance/processes to the context. It is expected — too few processes are ineffective, too many are wasteful.

questions.stem: What is the FIRST step of the four-step tailoring process?
options: Select the initial development approach using a suitability filter
questions.explanation: Step 1: select the initial development approach (suitability filter). Then tailor for the organization, then the project, then improve continuously.

questions.stem: Under which contract type does the SELLER bear the greatest cost risk?
questions.explanation: In fixed-price contracts the seller absorbs cost overruns, bearing the most cost risk. Cost-reimbursable shifts risk to the buyer.

questions.source: Study Guide Appendix X4; Glossary 'bid documents'
questions.stem: A buyer needs detailed proposals describing how sellers would solve a complex problem. Which solicitation document fits best?
questions.explanation: An RFP solicits full proposals/solutions for complex needs. RFQ is for price, RFI for information, IFB for sealed-bid commodity buys.

questions.stem: A make-or-buy analysis primarily supports which decision?
options: Whether to crash or fast-track the schedule
Whether to insource or outsource work
questions.explanation: Make-or-buy analysis informs insource vs. outsource decisions, weighing cost, skills, risk, schedule, and culture.

questions.stem: Which PMO type exercises the HIGHEST degree of control, even managing projects directly?
questions.explanation: Directive PMOs manage projects directly (high control). Supportive = low (templates), Controlling = moderate (compliance).

questions.stem: An AI tool generates a risk-response recommendation. According to the 8th Edition, who remains responsible for the decision?
options: No one, since AI made it
questions.explanation: Project managers remain responsible for AI-generated recommendations; AI assists but does not absolve human accountability.

questions.stem: Which trio describes the 8th Edition's three AI strategies across domains?
questions.explanation: The three AI strategies are Automation (AI executes), Assistance (AI supports decisions), and Augmentation (AI enhances capability).

questions.stem: Manage Project Knowledge addresses two kinds of knowledge. Which pair is correct?
options: Explicit (codifiable) and tacit (personal, hard to articulate)
questions.explanation: Explicit knowledge can be codified; tacit knowledge is personal and hard to articulate. Managing both builds organizational learning.

questions.stem: A project's deliverables have been accepted and operations has taken over. Which activity must the PM still ensure is completed before closing?
options: Document lessons learned and archive project records
questions.explanation: Closing includes documenting lessons learned, archiving records, and releasing resources — knowledge capture is essential.

questions.stem: Confirming a deliverable meets the customer's actual NEEDS (the right product) is:
questions.explanation: Validation = meeting the customer's needs (right product). Verification = complying with a spec/requirement (built right).

questions.stem: Investing in prevention and appraisal rather than dealing with failures reflects which concept?
questions.explanation: Cost of Quality weighs conformance (prevention + appraisal) against non-conformance (internal/external failure). Prevention is cheaper. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: The product owner wants to reorder upcoming work to reflect new market priorities. What is the appropriate mechanism?
options: Submit a formal change request to the CCB
questions.explanation: In adaptive work, changing priorities is handled by re-prioritizing the backlog — no formal CCB change request is needed.

questions.stem: During a retrospective the team raises that a recurring process problem keeps lowering velocity. What is the BEST outcome of the retrospective?
options: Assign blame to the slowest team member
Agree on a concrete improvement action to try next iteration
Cancel future retrospectives to save time
questions.explanation: Retrospectives drive continuous improvement by committing to actionable changes (inspect and adapt), not blame. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: Activity B cannot start until 3 days after Activity A finishes due to curing time. This forced delay is a:
questions.explanation: A required delay between activities is a lag. A lead is an overlap that lets a successor start early.

questions.stem: Planning near-term work in detail while leaving future work at a higher level, refining as you go, is called:
questions.explanation: Rolling wave planning is progressive elaboration of the schedule — detail the imminent work, elaborate later work as information emerges.

questions.stem: Purchasing long-lived equipment that becomes a capital asset is an example of:
questions.explanation: Capital expenditures (CapEx) fund long-term assets; operational expenditures (OpEx) cover day-to-day running costs.

questions.stem: Which estimating technique uses a statistical relationship such as cost-per-unit multiplied by quantity?
questions.explanation: Parametric estimating uses a rate/relationship (e.g., $/sq-ft × area). Analogous is historical/top-down; bottom-up sums work packages.

questions.stem: A high-performing team member's output has dropped sharply and they seem disengaged. What should the PM do FIRST?
options: Have a private one-on-one conversation to understand what's going on
questions.explanation: Lead with empathy: a private conversation uncovers root causes before any formal action. People-domain leadership. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: A PM wants to sustain intrinsic motivation on a knowledge-work team. Which approach is most aligned with that goal?
options: Tie every task to a cash bonus
Increase monitoring and status reporting frequency
questions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation for knowledge workers is driven by autonomy, mastery, and purpose rather than purely extrinsic rewards. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: Stakeholders complain they are not getting the information they need at the right time. What should the PM review and adjust?
questions.explanation: Communication gaps are addressed by revisiting the communications management plan — who needs what, when, how, and in what format.

questions.stem: Which communication method is MOST effective for resolving a sensitive interpersonal conflict?
options: A posted memo on the information radiator
questions.explanation: Interactive, face-to-face communication best handles sensitive or complex matters because it allows immediate, two-way feedback and reads tone. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: A shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking is known as:
questions.explanation: Psychological safety lets people speak up without fear; it underpins the Build an Empowered Culture principle.

questions.stem: A distributed team in five time zones struggles with collaboration. Which is the BEST first improvement?
options: Establish team agreements on core overlap hours and communication norms
Replace the team with a colocated one
questions.explanation: For virtual teams, agreed ground rules (core hours, channels, response norms) improve collaboration without unrealistic measures like relocation.

questions.stem: A previously identified risk's warning signs are now appearing. What should the PM do?
options: Wait until it becomes an issue, then react
Implement the planned risk response and continue monitoring
Remove it from the risk register
Escalate every risk to the sponsor
questions.explanation: When a trigger appears, execute the pre-planned response (Implement Risk Responses) and keep monitoring effectiveness.

questions.stem: Identify Risks is best characterized as:
options: An iterative process performed throughout the project
Performed only after risks become issues
questions.explanation: Risk identification is iterative — new risks emerge as the project evolves, so it recurs throughout the life cycle.

questions.stem: On the critical path, what is the total float of the activities?
questions.explanation: Critical path activities have zero total float — any delay extends the project end date.

questions.stem: In which organizational structure does the project manager typically have the LEAST authority?
questions.explanation: PM authority is lowest in functional structures and highest in project-oriented ones; matrix structures fall in between.

questions.stem: Which document justifies the project investment and is an input at initiation?
questions.explanation: The business case justifies the investment and is an input that the charter references; it is not produced by the project.

questions.stem: Which artifact defines the expected benefits and how/when they will be realized and measured?
questions.explanation: The benefits management plan documents target benefits, timing, metrics, and owners for realization.

questions.stem: A requested feature is technically elegant but delivers no measurable stakeholder value. Applying the principles, the PM should:
options: Build it because the team finds it interesting
Challenge it and prioritize work that delivers value
Add it silently to scope (gold plating)
questions.explanation: Focus on Value: every decision should maximize value. Building non-value work (or gold plating) contradicts the principle.

questions.stem: Which output consolidates all domain plans into the single authoritative reference for execution?
questions.explanation: Integrate and Align Project Plans produces the project management plan — the unified, authoritative reference for execution, M&C, and closing.

questions.stem: Limiting work in progress (WIP) on a Kanban board primarily helps to:
options: Increase the number of simultaneous tasks
Replace the need for a product owner
questions.explanation: WIP limits surface bottlenecks and overcommitment, optimizing flow through the system.

questions.stem: Which is the FIRST stakeholder process, producing the stakeholder register?
questions.explanation: Identify Stakeholders comes first and produces the stakeholder register, which feeds engagement planning.

questions.stem: A team member lacks a skill critical to upcoming work. What is the PM's BEST proactive response?
options: Remove them from the project immediately
Arrange coaching, mentoring, or training to develop the skill
Do the work yourself to avoid delay
questions.explanation: Developing the team (coaching, mentoring, training) builds capability and aligns with Lead the Team responsibilities.

questions.stem: A team identifies an opportunity and acts to make sure it definitely occurs. This strategy is:
questions.explanation: Exploit aims to ensure the opportunity happens (e.g., assigning the best resources). Enhance only increases probability/impact.

questions.stem: An adaptive team's average completed work per iteration, used to forecast future iterations, is its:
questions.explanation: Velocity is the average work (e.g., story points) completed per iteration, used for forecasting — not for comparing teams. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: What does Budget at Completion (BAC) represent?
options: The actual cost incurred so far
The sum of all budgets established for the work to be performed
The forecast final cost given current performance
questions.explanation: BAC is the total planned budget — the sum of all budgets for the work. EAC is the forecast final cost.

questions.stem: The principle 'Be an Accountable Leader' emphasizes that leaders should primarily:
options: Direct and command the team to ensure control
Own outcomes while facilitating, mentoring, and enabling the team
Delegate all accountability to team members
Avoid decisions to empower the team
questions.explanation: Accountable leaders own outcomes and serve the team by facilitating and enabling — not pure command-and-control, nor abdication.

questions.stem: A new influential stakeholder is discovered late in execution. What should the PM do FIRST?
options: Ignore them since planning is complete
Add them to the stakeholder register and analyze their interest/influence
questions.explanation: Identify Stakeholders is iterative; add and analyze the new stakeholder, then plan appropriate engagement.

questions.stem: Two team members have an ongoing personality clash hurting collaboration. As a facilitator, the PM should FIRST:
options: Separate them permanently with no discussion
Facilitate a direct conversation to surface and resolve the underlying issue
Let the team handle it with no involvement
questions.explanation: The PM facilitates resolution by getting the parties to address the root issue directly — collaboration over avoidance. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: Which Focus Area runs in parallel with all the others rather than as a standalone phase?
questions.explanation: Monitoring & Controlling runs continuously in parallel with the other Focus Areas; it is not a discrete phase.

questions.stem: Which artifact links each requirement to its source and to the deliverable that satisfies it?
questions.explanation: The requirements traceability matrix traces requirements from origin to deliverable, ensuring each is addressed and tested.

questions.stem: Which describes overall project risk?
options: A single high-priority risk in the register
The combined effect of all uncertainties on the project as a whole
The same as a residual risk
questions.explanation: Overall project risk is the aggregate effect of all uncertainties on the project; it can even trigger cancellation.

questions.stem: A dependency that is contractually required or inherent in the nature of the work is a:
questions.explanation: Mandatory (hard) dependencies are contractually required or physically inherent. Discretionary dependencies are preferred-logic choices.

questions.stem: Aggregating estimated costs of work packages into an authorized, time-phased cost baseline is the purpose of which process?
questions.explanation: Develop Budget aggregates cost estimates into the authorized, time-phased cost baseline.

questions.stem: Disciplined Agile (DA) is best described as:
options: A toolkit that helps teams choose their way of working
questions.explanation: DA is a process-decision toolkit (not a single prescriptive method) that helps teams tailor their way of working.

questions.stem: Manage Quality Assurance primarily focuses on:
options: Systematic activities that build confidence in the quality of processes and future outputs
questions.explanation: Quality Assurance is process-focused — systematic activities that build confidence in future outputs. Control Quality (QC) inspects deliverables.

questions.stem: An external impediment is blocking the team and is outside the PM's authority to resolve. After attempting to remove it, what is appropriate?
options: Escalate to the sponsor or appropriate authority
Blame the external team in the status report
questions.explanation: After trying to resolve it, escalate impediments beyond your authority to the sponsor/appropriate party. Escalation follows facilitation.

questions.stem: Which statement about stakeholder categories is correct?
options: Supportive, neutral, AND opposing stakeholders all require engagement
Neutral stakeholders can always be ignored
questions.explanation: All categories — supportive, neutral, and opposing — must be engaged, since unaddressed opposition or neutrality can derail value delivery.

questions.stem: Which two principles make up the 'Ownership' dimension of the PM mindset?
options: Adopt a Holistic View + Embed Quality
Be an Accountable Leader + Build an Empowered Culture
questions.explanation: Ownership = Be an Accountable Leader + Build an Empowered Culture. Proactive = Holistic View + Embed Quality. Value-Driven = Focus on Value + Integrate Sustainability.

questions.stem: Which describes the cause-event-consequence structure used to write a risk?
options: A structured statement: because of [cause], [event] may occur, leading to [consequence]
questions.explanation: Risks are well-stated as cause → uncertain event → consequence, clarifying the source and effect on objectives.

questions.stem: An adaptive team is asked to produce a detailed up-front Gantt chart for the whole year. The BEST response is to:
options: Comply and freeze the full-year plan
Explain that adaptive work uses timeboxed iterations and a roadmap, and plan progressively
Convert the project to fully predictive
questions.explanation: Adaptive scheduling favors a roadmap plus timeboxed iterations and progressive elaboration over a rigid full-year Gantt chart.

questions.stem: Partway through, market changes mean the project will no longer deliver its expected business value. What should the PM do?
options: Continue to completion regardless, to avoid waste
Raise the issue so leadership can decide whether to pivot or terminate the project
Quietly reduce quality to finish faster
Ignore it; value is the sponsor's concern only
questions.explanation: Focus on Value: if value is no longer attainable, surface it so governance can decide to pivot, re-scope, or terminate. Sponsors can propose termination.

questions.stem: Which governance component provides 'early warning' of future problems before tolerances are breached?
questions.explanation: Leading indicators and signaling mechanisms warn early; lagging indicators report after the fact.

questions.stem: A team member quietly adds extra unrequested features they think the customer will like. This is an example of, and should be handled by:
options: Gold plating; reinforce delivering only approved scope
questions.explanation: Adding unrequested extras is gold plating — it wastes effort and adds risk. Deliver only approved scope; route real changes through control.

questions.stem: A hierarchical list of resources organized by category and type is a:
questions.explanation: The resource breakdown structure (RBS) categorizes resources by type/category; it is an output of Estimate Resources.

questions.stem: Which output defines risk categories, probability/impact scales, and stakeholder risk tolerances?
questions.explanation: The risk management plan sets the approach: categories, scales, roles, tolerances, and register format. The register lists actual risks.

questions.source: Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix levels per PMI standard (matrix named in Study Guide §3.5; level labels not in the guide — verify)
questions.stem: Which is a valid engagement level on the Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix?
questions.explanation: Levels are Unaware, Resistant, Neutral, Supportive, and Leading. 'Definitive' and 'salient' belong to the Salience model.

questions.stem: EV = 500, PV = 400, AC = 450. Which statement is TRUE?
options: Ahead of schedule and under budget (SPI>1, CPI>1)
Ahead of schedule but over budget
questions.explanation: SPI = 500/400 = 1.25 (>1, ahead). CPI = 500/450 ≈ 1.11 (>1, under budget). Both favorable.

questions.stem: Stakeholders pressure an agile team to add scope mid-sprint. The team's best response is to:
options: Immediately add it to the current sprint
Protect the sprint goal and place the request in the backlog for prioritization
questions.explanation: Protect the current sprint commitment; route new requests to the backlog where the product owner re-prioritizes for a future sprint. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: Which log tracks current conditions requiring action that have already occurred?
questions.explanation: The issue log records current conditions (already occurred) needing action. Risks (potential) go in the risk register.

questions.stem: Which is a data-gathering technique for eliciting requirements?
questions.explanation: Focus groups (and interviews, brainstorming, surveys) are data-gathering techniques for requirements elicitation.

questions.stem: A vendor and your team disagree on a deliverable's interpretation. The MOST collaborative first step is to:
options: Negotiate toward a mutually acceptable interpretation based on the contract intent
questions.explanation: Start with good-faith negotiation toward a win-win; claims/termination are later, more adversarial steps. (ECO-extended.)

questions.stem: A tornado diagram is the output of which technique?
questions.explanation: Sensitivity analysis is displayed as a tornado diagram, ranking which variables most affect the outcome.

questions.stem: Which technique models schedule uncertainty by running many iterations to produce a probability distribution of completion dates?
questions.explanation: Monte Carlo simulation runs many iterations to yield a probability distribution of outcomes (e.g., finish dates or cost).

questions.stem: 'Adopt a Holistic View' primarily guards against which problem?
questions.explanation: A holistic, systems-thinking view considers components and interdependencies, preventing siloed decisions.

questions.stem: The 'triple bottom line' framework evaluates a business across which three dimensions?
questions.explanation: Triple bottom line = Profit, People, Planet — the basis for sustainability value.

questions.stem: Quality in the 8th Edition is treated as:
options: An integral part of the Scope performance domain
questions.explanation: In the 8th Edition, quality is encompassed within the Scope performance domain — functional and non-functional requirements together.

questions.stem: Before work begins, the PM wants the team to agree on how they will work together and handle decisions. The BEST tool is to create a:
questions.explanation: A team charter, co-created with the team, sets working agreements, norms, and decision-making expectations up front.

questions.stem: An adaptive team uses daily standups partly as a governance mechanism. This reflects which governance model?
questions.explanation: Self-governance gives the team autonomy with feedback mechanisms like daily standups; structured governance relies on boards and the PMO.

questions.stem: During analysis the team realizes an item labeled a 'risk' has actually already happened and is affecting work now. They should:
options: Keep it in the risk register
Move it to the issue log and address it
Escalate it to the CCB as a change
questions.explanation: An event that has already occurred is an issue, not a risk — track it in the issue log and act on it.

questions.stem: Which is an example of TANGIBLE project value?
questions.explanation: ROI/IRR/payback are tangible (quantifiable financial) value; reputation, morale, and social impact are intangible.

questions.stem: Refining and re-ordering items, adding detail and estimates to upcoming backlog items, is called:
questions.explanation: Backlog refinement keeps upcoming items detailed, estimated, and prioritized so the team can plan iterations effectively.

questions.stem: An executive wants only high-level summaries while the technical lead needs detailed data. The PM should:
options: Send everyone the same full report
Tailor communication content, format, and frequency to each stakeholder's needs
questions.explanation: Communication should be tailored to each stakeholder's needs — channel, format, detail, and frequency vary by audience.

questions.stem: A team performs two normally-sequential activities partially in parallel to save time, accepting added rework risk. This is:
questions.explanation: Fast tracking overlaps sequential activities to compress the schedule, increasing risk of rework. Crashing adds resources/cost instead.

questions.stem: A change request is approved by the CCB. What should the PM do next?
options: Update the affected baselines/plans and implement the change
Add it to the lessons learned register and stop
questions.explanation: Once approved, update the affected plans/baselines and implement the change, then communicate it via the change log.

questions.stem: Which best captures a central shift in the 8th Edition's definition of a project?
options: From 'temporary endeavor' to 'temporary initiative in a unique context to create value'
From adaptive to predictive as the default
questions.explanation: The 8th Edition redefines a project as a 'temporary initiative in a unique context undertaken to create value,' emphasizing value creation.

questions.stem: A team member consistently misses commitments. As a servant-leader, what should the PM do FIRST?
options: Reassign their work to higher performers
Document it for a formal review
Have a private conversation to understand obstacles and offer support
Raise it publicly in the next standup to create accountability
questions.explanation: Lead with a private, supportive conversation to surface root causes before formal or public measures.

questions.stem: Which is the BEST example of active listening during a stakeholder meeting?
options: Planning your rebuttal while they speak
Paraphrasing their point back to confirm understanding before responding
Taking the floor to share your own view
questions.explanation: Active listening means fully attending and reflecting/paraphrasing to confirm understanding before responding.

questions.stem: A globally distributed team feels disconnected and trust is low. Which action best builds team cohesion?
options: Increase the number of status reports
Hold a virtual kickoff and create team agreements plus informal connection time
Replace remote members with local hires
questions.explanation: Cohesion grows from shared agreements, a kickoff, and deliberate relationship-building, not heavier reporting.

questions.stem: Using the Thomas-Kilmann modes, which approach yields a lose-lose outcome and is generally least desirable?
questions.explanation: Avoiding/withdrawing defers the conflict and resolves nothing — generally the least effective mode.

questions.stem: A high performer says they feel stagnant and unchallenged. The BEST retention-oriented response is to:
options: Provide growth: stretch assignments, mentoring, or new responsibilities
Ignore it; technical work speaks for itself
questions.explanation: Mastery and growth opportunities sustain intrinsic motivation for knowledge workers better than one-off rewards.

questions.stem: During a conflict between two team members, the PM facilitates a discussion where both openly explore the problem and reach a solution both support. This is:
questions.explanation: Both parties openly working to a mutually supported solution is Collaborate/Problem-solve — the win-win mode.

questions.stem: A new team is in the Storming stage. Which leadership behavior is MOST appropriate?
options: Step back entirely and let them self-organize
Coach: facilitate resolving conflicts and clarify roles and goals
questions.explanation: In Storming the leader actively coaches — facilitating conflict resolution and clarifying roles — to move toward Norming.

questions.stem: Which behavior best demonstrates emotional intelligence in a tense project review?
options: Suppressing all emotion and ignoring the room
Recognizing your own and others' emotions and responding with composure and empathy
Matching a frustrated stakeholder's raised voice
Ending the meeting to avoid discomfort
questions.explanation: EI is recognizing and managing one's own and others' emotions, responding with self-control and empathy.

questions.stem: A stakeholder with no formal authority over the team keeps influencing priorities informally. The PM should:
options: Understand their interests and use influencing skills to align them with project goals
Ignore them since they lack authority
questions.explanation: Influence without authority is managed by understanding interests and engaging/influencing toward shared goals.

questions.stem: An empowered, self-organizing team asks the PM to stop assigning their daily tasks. The PM should:
options: Refuse; assigning tasks is the PM's job
Support self-organization and shift to removing impediments and coaching
Escalate the request to the sponsor
Assign even more tasks to maintain control
questions.explanation: Empowered teams self-assign; the leader's role shifts to enabling, coaching, and clearing impediments.

questions.stem: A team member from a different culture stays silent in meetings but contributes well one-on-one. The PM should:
options: Pressure them to speak up publicly or be removed
Adapt: create inclusive channels (written input, smaller groups) that respect diverse styles
questions.explanation: Cultural and communication-style diversity is an asset; adapt engagement methods to be inclusive rather than forcing one style.

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY purpose of giving timely, specific feedback to team members?
options: To build a paper trail for performance reviews
To reinforce good performance and enable course-correction while it still matters
To compare team members against each other
questions.explanation: Timely, specific feedback reinforces strengths and enables correction when it can still change the outcome.

questions.stem: Two senior stakeholders escalate a disagreement to the PM, each demanding the PM side with them. The BEST first step is to:
options: Side with the more powerful stakeholder
Facilitate a joint conversation focused on shared project objectives and data
Defer the decision to the sponsor immediately
Let them resolve it without involvement
questions.explanation: Facilitate toward shared objectives using data before escalating; the PM acts as a neutral facilitator.

questions.stem: A team consistently hits its commitments but never raises problems until they become crises. This most likely indicates a lack of:
questions.explanation: When people won't surface problems early, psychological safety is missing — they don't feel safe taking interpersonal risk.

questions.stem: The PM notices a skills gap that will hurt an upcoming phase. The MOST proactive leadership response is to:
options: Wait until the gap causes a delay, then react
Arrange training, pairing, or mentoring now to develop the capability
questions.explanation: Developing the team proactively (training, pairing, mentoring) addresses the gap before it impacts delivery.

questions.stem: An agile team's daily standup has turned into a 40-minute status report to the PM. The servant-leader should:
options: Keep it; more detail is always better
Refocus it on the team's coordination (plan/blockers) and timebox it, taking deep-dives offline
questions.explanation: Standups are for the team to self-coordinate and surface blockers within a timebox — not a report to the PM.

questions.stem: A stakeholder repeatedly changes requirements late, frustrating the team. The PM's BEST people-focused response is to:
options: Engage the stakeholder to understand drivers and agree on how changes will be handled collaboratively
Vent the team's frustration to the sponsor
questions.explanation: Engage to understand the underlying need and co-create a workable change approach — relationship-first problem solving.

questions.stem: Which best reflects appropriate delegation by a project manager?
options: Assigning a task and then doing it yourself anyway
Matching the task to the person's capability, granting authority, and supporting them
Delegating only the work you dislike
Delegating accountability for outcomes entirely away
questions.explanation: Effective delegation matches task to capability, grants the authority to act, and provides support; the PM still owns the outcome.

questions.stem: A remote team member is being overlooked for recognition compared to colocated peers. The PM should:
options: Ensure recognition is equitable and visible regardless of location
Stop recognizing anyone to be fair
Let the team sort it out
questions.explanation: Equitable, visible recognition across locations sustains motivation and fairness on hybrid/virtual teams.

questions.stem: The team disagrees on a technical approach and asks the PM to decide. With time available and the team closest to the work, the BEST move is to:
options: Make the call yourself to save time
Facilitate the team to a data-informed decision they own
questions.explanation: When time allows and the team has the expertise, facilitate their ownership of the decision rather than deciding for them.
